title: What a quiet library does to a crowd
summary: Describe how the atmosphere of a public library changes the behaviour of strangers near you, and argue whether silent shared spaces are disappearing too fast.
topic: The effect of shared silence in a public library
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) describing how the atmosphere of a library changes the way strangers behave near each other. Use specific details to describe what you notice. Then argue whether cities should protect quiet shared spaces like libraries, using modals and present perfect.
hintStarters: The moment I step inside, I notice that…
What has always impressed me about a library is the way…
If cities allowed more spaces like this to close, we would lose…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm thấy thoải mái, thư thái
bóp nghẹt âm thanh, giảm tiếng ồn
tiếng vo ve của điều hòa không khí

hintVocab.phrase: to be on your best behaviour
hintVocab.meaningVi: cư xử tốt nhất có thể
tài trợ cho một dịch vụ công
nơi trú ẩn khỏi tiếng ồn
quan sát mà không nói chuyện

sampleText:
On a Tuesday afternoon, the main public library in Lyon was half-full of people who had nothing in common except that they had all come to be quiet. There was a teenager with headphones around her neck, not in her ears, reading a geography textbook. There was a man in a delivery jacket who had fallen asleep over a newspaper — not through boredom, it seemed, but through exhaustion, the kind that only looks peaceful from the outside. There was a woman marking up a printed script with a yellow pen, mouthing the words without making a sound. None of them knew each other, but they were all operating under the same unspoken agreement: whatever is happening outside, in here we do not disturb.

What this silence has done, over centuries of practice, is create a particular kind of social permission. In a library, you are allowed to sit next to a stranger for two hours without ever speaking to them, and neither of you will feel this is strange. You can observe someone and they can observe you, and there is no pressure to perform or explain your presence. The shared silence is not cold — it is the closest thing I have found to being alone in public without being isolated. People tend to become slightly more careful with their movements: they fold their bags under the chair, they step around someone crouching at a low shelf, they carry their coffee cups with two hands. The space asks for consideration, and most people give it, as if they understand, on arrival, that the library is making a request rather than imposing a rule.

Many cities have been closing library branches over the past decade, replacing them with digital services on the grounds that people can access books online. This argument misunderstands what a library actually does. An e-reader can provide a book; it cannot provide the feeling of working quietly beside someone who is also working quietly. The social function of the library — the thing it has been doing for its neighbourhood for generations — is not lending books. It is lending a room where the rules are already agreed. If that room disappears, the people who needed it most are often those with the least space at home: students sharing a flat, workers without a desk, anyone who has come to realise that concentration is easier when other people are also concentrating nearby. A library should not have to justify its existence in terms of borrowing statistics.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to describe a lasting social effect
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What this silence has done, over centuries of practice, is create a particular kind of social permission' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt tác động tích lũy của thực hành lâu dài. Cấu trúc 'What … has done … is + infinitive' là cách nhấn mạnh kết quả một cách tự nhiên trong tiếng Anh B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What this silence has done, over centuries of practice, is create a particular kind of social permission' uses the present perfect to describe the accumulated effect of a long practice. The structure 'What … has done … is + infinitive' is a natural B1 way to emphasise a result.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'can' to describe general social permission
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'You can observe someone and they can observe you' dùng 'can' để mô tả điều được phép hoặc có thể xảy ra trong ngữ cảnh xã hội — không phải khả năng vật lý mà là sự cho phép ngầm hiểu trong không gian đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'You can observe someone and they can observe you' uses 'can' to describe what is socially permitted — not physical ability, but implicit permission within that space.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào một buổi chiều thứ Ba, thư viện công cộng chính ở Lyon nửa đầy người không có điểm gì chung ngoại trừ việc họ đều đến để được yên tĩnh. Có một thiếu niên với tai nghe đeo quanh cổ, không phải trong tai, đang đọc sách giáo khoa địa lý. Có một người đàn ông mặc áo khoác giao hàng đã ngủ gục trên tờ báo. Có một người phụ nữ đánh dấu trên một bản in với bút màu vàng, mấp máy môi mà không phát ra âm thanh. Không ai trong số họ biết nhau, nhưng tất cả đều hành động theo cùng một thỏa thuận ngầm: dù bên ngoài có chuyện gì xảy ra, ở đây chúng ta không quấy rầy.

Điều mà sự im lặng này đã làm, qua nhiều thế kỷ thực hành, là tạo ra một loại sự cho phép xã hội đặc biệt. Trong thư viện, bạn được phép ngồi cạnh một người lạ suốt hai tiếng mà không bao giờ nói chuyện với họ, và không ai trong hai người cảm thấy điều này kỳ lạ. Bạn có thể quan sát ai đó và họ có thể quan sát bạn, và không có áp lực phải thể hiện hay giải thích sự hiện diện của bạn. Sự im lặng chung không lạnh lùng — đó là điều gần nhất tôi tìm thấy với việc một mình ở nơi công cộng mà không cảm thấy cô lập. Mọi người có xu hướng cẩn thận hơn một chút với cử động của họ: họ gấp túi xách dưới ghế, họ bước vòng qua người đang cúi xuống ở kệ sách thấp, họ cầm cốc cà phê bằng hai tay. Không gian đòi hỏi sự chu đáo, và hầu hết mọi người đều đáp lại điều đó.

Nhiều thành phố đã đóng cửa các chi nhánh thư viện trong thập kỷ qua, thay thế chúng bằng dịch vụ kỹ thuật số với lý do người ta có thể truy cập sách trực tuyến. Lập luận này hiểu sai điều mà một thư viện thực sự làm. Một thiết bị đọc sách điện tử có thể cung cấp một cuốn sách; nó không thể cung cấp cảm giác làm việc yên lặng bên cạnh ai đó cũng đang làm việc yên lặng. Chức năng xã hội của thư viện — điều nó đã làm cho khu phố của mình qua nhiều thế hệ — không phải là cho mượn sách. Đó là cho mượn một căn phòng mà các quy tắc đã được thống nhất. Nếu căn phòng đó biến mất, những người cần nó nhất thường là những người có ít không gian nhất ở nhà: sinh viên chung căn hộ, người lao động không có bàn làm việc, bất kỳ ai đã nhận ra rằng khả năng tập trung dễ dàng hơn khi những người khác xung quanh cũng đang tập trung. Một thư viện không nên phải biện minh cho sự tồn tại của mình bằng số liệu thống kê mượn sách.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'nothing in common' in a relative clause?
options: People who had nothing in common except that they had all come to be quiet.
People who had nothing in common except they have all come to be quiet.
People who have nothing in common except that they all come to be quiet.
People who had nothing common except that they had all come to be quiet.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Nothing in common' is the fixed phrase — 'nothing common' omits the required preposition. The narrative is past, so 'had come' (past perfect) is correct. Present perfect 'have all come' is inconsistent with past narrative tense.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'nothing in common', not 'nothing common'.

options: Neither of you will feel this is strange.
Neither of you will feel this is stranger.
Neither of you will feel this be strange.
Neither of you feel this is strange.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither of you will feel + that clause' is standard. 'Stranger' is comparative and doesn't fit. 'This be strange' is a subjunctive form, unusual here. Without 'will', the sentence uses present simple, which could work but changes the modality.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Neither of you will' uses a modal to make a forward prediction about both people.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'tend to' followed by a comparative?
options: People tend to become slightly more careful with their movements.
People tend becoming slightly more careful with their movements.
People tend to become slightly more carefully with their movements.
People are tending to become slightly more careful with their movements.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + bare infinitive' is correct. 'Tend becoming' drops the 'to'. 'More carefully' is an adverb but the complement of 'become' needs an adjective ('careful'). 'Are tending' — 'tend' as a stative-like verb rarely uses the progressive.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'tend to', use the base verb; the complement of 'become' is an adjective, not an adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'allow' + object + infinitive correctly?
options: If cities allowed more spaces like this to close, we would lose something.
If cities allowed more spaces like this closing, we would lose something.
If cities allow more spaces like this to close, we would lose something.
If cities allowed more spaces like this to be closing, we would lose something.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Allow + object + to + infinitive' is the standard pattern. 'Allow + object + -ing' is used in some dialects but less formal. 'Cities allow' (present) with 'would' breaks the second conditional pattern. 'To be closing' (passive progressive) is unnecessarily complex.
mcQuestions.hint: The pattern 'allow + object + to + base verb' is the formal standard.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses 'while' to contrast two simultaneous states?
options: She was mouthing the words while not making a sound.
She mouthed the words while not making a sound.
She was mouthing the words while she was not making a sound.
She mouthed the words while she not made a sound.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple with a 'while' clause using a participial phrase ('while not making') is clean and natural for narrative. Past progressive in both clauses ('was mouthing… was not making') is grammatically correct but heavier. 'While she not made' is missing the auxiliary 'did'.
mcQuestions.hint: In narrative past, a participial clause ('while not making') is leaner than a full second clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'closest' as a superlative in a comparison?
options: It is the closest thing I have found to being alone in public.
It is the most close thing I have found to being alone in public.
It is the closer thing I have found to being alone in public.
It is the closet thing I have found to being alone in public.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Closest' is the irregular superlative of 'close'. 'Most close' is non-standard — short adjectives form superlatives with '-est'. 'Closer' is comparative, not superlative. 'Closet' is a spelling error (a small room).
mcQuestions.hint: Short adjectives form the superlative by adding '-est', not 'most + adjective'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'carry' with two complements correctly?
options: They carry their coffee cups with two hands.
They carry their coffee cups by two hands.
They carry their coffee cups in two hands.
They carry their coffee cups on two hands.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'With two hands' is the correct preposition phrase for how you hold something. 'By two hands' suggests means (as in 'caught by the hands'). 'In two hands' is unusual for carrying. 'On two hands' is incorrect in this sense.
mcQuestions.hint: The correct preposition for describing how you hold or carry something using your hands is 'with'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whatever' correctly as a concessive conjunction?
options: Whatever is happening outside, in here we do not disturb.
Whatever happens outside, in here we don't disturb.
Whatever is happening outside, in here we are not disturbing.
Whatsoever is happening outside, in here we do not disturb.
mcQuestions.explanation: All options are close. Option A uses present progressive for 'what is happening' (ongoing), which is natural for an unspoken social rule. Option B also works but uses present simple 'happens', which is slightly more general and formal. Option C uses progressive for the rule 'we are not disturbing', which sounds like a temporary state. 'Whatsoever' is too emphatic.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whatever is happening' emphasises the ongoing, unknown nature of outside events — this matches the unspoken rule context.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly describes what someone was doing in the library?
options: A man in a delivery jacket had fallen asleep over a newspaper.
A man in a delivery jacket has fallen asleep over a newspaper.
A man in a delivery jacket fallen asleep over a newspaper.
A man in a delivery jacket was fallen asleep over a newspaper.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had fallen asleep' describes a state that was already true at the time of observation (narrative past). Present perfect 'has fallen' connects to now, inappropriate in a past narrative. 'Fallen' alone has no auxiliary. 'Was fallen' incorrectly uses passive structure with an intransitive verb.
mcQuestions.hint: In past narrative, use past perfect to describe a state that was already the case at the observed moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no pressure to' correctly with a verb?
options: There is no pressure to perform or explain your presence.
There is no pressure of performing or explaining your presence.
There is no pressure for perform or explain your presence.
There is no pressure about performing or explaining your presence.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No pressure to + infinitive' is the standard construction. 'No pressure of + gerund' and 'no pressure about + gerund' are non-standard preposition choices. 'No pressure for + base verb' is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'no pressure to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'step around' as a phrasal verb?
options: They step around someone crouching at a low shelf.
They step around to someone crouching at a low shelf.
They step around someone who is crouching on a low shelf.
They step around someone crouching on a low shelf.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Step around + object' means to move around someone/something to avoid them. 'Step around to' changes the meaning (to go over to someone). 'At a low shelf' (being near or beside) is more natural than 'on a shelf' (being on top of it). The participial 'crouching' and relative clause 'who is crouching' are both grammatical, but the participial form is leaner.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the person 'at' (beside) the shelf or 'on' (on top of) the shelf? And does 'step around to' mean the same as 'step around'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: A library offers silence you did not create and did not have to negotiate. Is there something different about a silence you share with strangers, compared to a silence you arrange for yourself at home?
